data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - August 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

During the last month, the U.S. administration has imposed new tariffs on a host of countries but also finalized some high-profile trade deals, especially with Japan, South Korea, and the European Union. In all three cases, the U.S. will impose a tariff of 15% while there would be no reciprocal tariff from the other side. Even though the devil is always in the details, it looks like a victory for the administration with three of the major trading partners. Also, it appears that the administration has moved from a base 10% tariff to a 15% tariff for most of the world. Also, the trade discussions with China are still ongoing, and the previous pause has been extended by another three months. Moreover, we are likely not going to see the kind of tensions we had back in April, as both sides seem to have understood the pitfalls. Obviously, the markets are liking what they are seeing. Also, the 2nd quarter earnings season has been going strong overall, with over 90% of companies in the S&P500 having reported already. The year-over-year “blended” earnings growth rate for the S&P 500 is 11.8% (so far), which is pretty good. The market is making new all-time highs almost on a regular basis.

Also, in the meantime, the July jobs report was dismal, and the previous two months were also revised downwards. That raised a lot of hope for the interest rate cut by the Fed in its next meeting. Obviously, further progress of the market and the broader economy will depend a great deal on what the Fed will do or not do in the coming months. However, the Fed has offered no hint of a rate cut during its next meeting, slated for September 2025. We have been saying for some time that the Fed is already late in lowering the rates, and the longer it waits, the greater the harm it may cause to the broader economy, the job market, and especially to the housing market. Nonetheless, the probability of a rate cut of 25 basis points (in Sep. FOMC) has gone above 90%. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, CI, CTRA, FIX, HWM, MLI, PWR, and RL (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: AVGO (2 duplicates).

We are now left with 40 (50 - 10) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes STZ, CDW, CI, OTIS, MOS, OVV, LLY, NUE, and AMH (leaving us 31 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes DKS and CINF, leaving us with 29 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. We remove CARR, RGLD, MSCI, WTRG, CTRA, and VOYA. After removing these six names, we are left with 23 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep BBVA, but remove IBN.

Finance-Regional Banks: We keep BK.

Finance – Inv. Banking and others: We will keep MS but remove V.

Computer Software: We will keep MSFT and INTU.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO, but remove TSM.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM.

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep PWR.

Utility: We keep NFG.

In all, we removed three names, so we are finally left with 20 (23-3) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(FIX), (BBVA), (BK), (INTU), (MSFT), (RL), (EME), (PWR), (APH), (MS), (CW), (HWM), (TRV), (MLI), (WPM), (FSV), (AVGO), (TS), (CTAS), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is 'not' a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Jul. 2025):

(FIX), (BBVA), (MSFT), (RL), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (WPM), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Previous Month Selections:

(FIX), (SAN), (RL), (PWR), (APH), (MS), (HWM), (GILD), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. However, this month, similar to past trends, six stocks were retained, and four stocks were replaced with new ones.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

After 8.8%, 12.5%, and 5.80% gains in the previous three months, the portfolio continued to excel this past month with gains of 8.56% and also topped both the S&P 500 and the benchmark, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG), by a wide margin.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from Jan. 2010 until Jul. 2025. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 10 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of Jan. 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

The portfolio had a very strong showing this month, gaining 8.56%, after having strong gains during the past three months. It was also ahead of the S&P500 by a wide margin. The S&P500 and our benchmark ETF, VIG, gained 2.86% and 1.16% in the same period, respectively. The star performers for this past month were Comfort Systems (FIX), Broadcom (AVGO), Amphenol (APH), and Banco Santander (SAN). We also had attractive returns from Gilead Sciences (GILD) and National Fuel Gas Co (NFG). However, Howmet Aerospace (HWM) had a slightly negative performance this past month after strong gains in the previous months. Many of these stocks, except Gilead, are repeating for the month of August. Also, SAN is being replaced with similar banking stock Banco Bilbao (BBVA).

This month, four stocks are being replaced with new ones, which is about average. The new entrants are Banco Bilbao (BBVA), Microsoft (MSFT), EMCOR Group (EME), and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM). The portfolio of 10 stocks is highly diversified into many different sectors and industry segments.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not buy recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially at the present time. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: 6 Stocks I'm Buying As Tariffs Weigh Down The Economy (Chart Storm!)
data.description:
Get ready for enough charts to make your eyes cross!

In last week's article, "How I've Build A Monster Passive Income Portfolio," I did not provide an update on the economy or markets, which means I now have two weeks' worth of charts to drop.

It's gonna be awesome.

Here are our topics de la semaine:

  • The tug-of-war between disinflationary housing and inflationary tariffs within the CPI.
  • Some charts showcasing the K-shaped economy, wherein the thriving affluent are taking up the slack from the surviving (or suffering) paycheck-to-paycheck class.
  • The stock market is K-shaped as well, bifurcated between the AI-related winners and almost everybody else.
  • Some comments on my buy list and dividend growth investing in this increasingly K-shaped world.

Onward!

Inflation: The Tug-Of-War Between Tariffs and Housing

There's a tug-of-war going on in the CPI right now.

On one hand, you've got housing/shelter, which has been in a solid disinflationary trend for the last few years. These lagged shelter CPI metrics will continue to decline as they catch up to flattish real-time housing inflation.

On the other hand, you've got the highest average effective tariff rate in a century, which a mountain of economic research (example: see here and here) suggests will trigger price increases, which in turn will fuel a temporary bump in inflation.

So, while the shelter component of the CPI is exerting downward pressure on the inflation index, tariffs are slowly but surely exerting upward pressure on it.

As I wrote in a recent article:

According to economists at Goldman Sachs (source), as of June, US importing businesses have absorbed about two-thirds of the tariff costs, while consumers have absorbed 22% of it via price increases and exporters have absorbed 14% via lower export prices.

However, as you can see above, the Goldman Sachs economists predict that by the final months of this year, two-thirds of tariff costs will be absorbed by consumers via price hikes.

If they are right, then the CPI should experience a roughly 50 basis point bump between now and the end of 2025.

(My own view is that it will take a year or more for businesses to pass through most of the tariff costs to consumers because of the financial fragility of most American consumers today, but I directionally agree with the Goldman Sachs assessment.)

This past week, we got some hard data that corroborates the Goldman Sachs data. Producer prices (paid by businesses) surged in July as tariffs have had sufficient time to begin rippling through the economy.

This represented the fastest spike in producer prices in three years.

Businesses appear to be passing through tariff costs to other businesses, even if they're being slow and deliberate about passing through the costs to final consumers.

But there are areas where businesses are already raising consumer prices due to the tariffs.

Quoting the article again:

Economists from Harvard and the University of San Andres have been tracking the pricing impact of the tariffs in real time and publishing their findings every two weeks. By looking at pricing data from four large US retailers, they have found noticeable, albeit gradual, price increases that have been triggered by the imposition of tariffs.

Domestic goods have also risen in price, partly because many domestically produced goods use imported raw materials or intermediate parts and partly because domestic goods producers have used tariff protections as cover to expand margins by raising prices.

The auto sector is another obvious area where tariff-induced inflation is coming - and, indeed, already happening.

Auto repair and maintenance and car parts and equipment are seeing prices spike at their sharpest levels in years.

YoY Car Maintenance/Repair (Blue) and YoY Car Parts and Equipment (Green):

Since auto parts are tariffed at the same rate as finished cars in most cases, it's unsurprising to see tariff costs flowing through to consumers faster for these essential goods and services than for big-ticket durable goods like cars.

Quoting the article one more time:

Auto experts have warned about coming price increases since early April.

Likewise, along with higher car and auto maintenance/repair/parts prices come higher car insurance premiums.

Car insurance aggregator Insurify estimates that car insurance premiums should increase between 5-10% across the country by the end of this year.

So far, we have only just seen the beginning of the total tariff-induced inflation to come.

Now, on the other end of the tug-of-war rope is a strong disinflationary trend in the oversupplied housing sector, which accounts for about 35% of headline CPI and over 40% of core CPI.

YoY Rent (Blue) and YoY Owners' Equivalent Rent (Green):

As previously stated, both of these shelter inflation metrics lag real-time changes in market rent rates by a year or more.

Given the fact that market rents have been flat for about two years and will likely remain flat for a while longer amid an oversupplied multifamily market and weak job growth, I expect this disinflationary trend in shelter CPI to continue for the foreseeable future.

Interestingly, if you substituted shelter CPI for real-time residential rent changes, the CPI would have been at or below the Fed's target rate for years now.

Every month, Jeremy Schwartz of WisdomTree puts together a chart showing what the CPI would be using real-time market rent data from private sector sources instead of the CPI's lagged shelter metrics. Real-time inflation has been at or lower than 2% for the past two years, and even after a slight uptick in June-July from tariffs, it remains below 2%.

These charts show that while real-time inflation is slowly rebounding, it still remains quite low. This is a major reason why most people aren't yet seeing or experiencing the inflation to come.

The CPI will eventually re-converge with real-time inflation, but it's difficult to say how long it will take to accomplish that. And it's difficult to say whether tariffs will cause another short-term divergence like the post-COVID inflationary surge did in 2021-2023.

All we can say is that for the foreseeable future, the shelter component will continue to exert downward pressure on the CPI.

Meanwhile, tariffs will continue to exert upward pressure on the CPI.

The K-Shaped Economy In 5 Charts

In last week's article, I briefly mentioned the K-shaped economy, and I've discussed this topic in many past articles.

This concept refers to the divergent directions that have been taken in recent times by the affluent (high-income, asset owners, high savings rate) and the paycheck-to-paycheck class (low- to moderate-income, low net worth, little to no monthly savings).

This divergence is necessary to understand why many consumers are hurting and falling behind on bills even as total consumption continues to power forward.

From 2021 through 2023, fueled by "stimmy" money and a post-COVID reopening, there was a broad-based advance in consumer spending, inclusive of the low- and middle-income groups.

But as you can see, since 2024, low- and middle-income consumption has only grown about as much as consumer prices have increased, meaning that real consumption has flattened for the majority of Americans.

Interestingly, the chart above indicates that nominal spending by the top quintile of income earners has flattened since the beginning of 2024, indicating that their real consumption is actually down.

Even so, it's rich Americans who have the greatest capacity to grow their real consumption.

While wage growth for the lowest quartile (bottom 25%) of income earners has now dropped to below pre-pandemic levels, wage growth for the highest quartile (highest 25%) of earners remains fixed at almost 5%, higher than any time except 2023 in decades.

Here's another breakout of wage growth by income group, zooming in on the last few years in order to capture the major shift from the post-COVID labor shortage years to today:

Perhaps the flattening of consumption by the rich over the last year or so (even as their wages continue growing) paradoxically helps to explain the impressive performance of the stock market. Instead of the incremental dollar going toward spending, maybe it is instead going into the capital markets and bidding up stock prices.

Here's a wrench to throw into the situation: Recent college graduates appear to be, by and large, joining the ranks of the paycheck-to-paycheck class rather than starting down the road to affluence.

The burgeoning advancement of artificial intelligence over the last few years has led businesses to dramatically cut the number of new college grads they hire. From the pre-pandemic years to the present environment, the biggest jump in unemployment has been seen among bachelor's degree holders. The second biggest jump comes from graduate degree holders.

Put simply, more and more knowledge-based work is now being done by AI tools, not people.

That has fueled a continuation in what has actually been a long-term trend in rising underemployment among recent college grads.

The bottom rung of the post-collegiate career ladder now appears to be too high for many graduates to reach, which means that the barista drawing a smiley face on your coffee cup is increasingly likely to have a bachelor's degree.

My personal opinion is that the age of encouraging almost every high schooler to go to college is probably over. The pendulum ought to (and, I think, will) swing back toward the legitimization and dignification of trade schools and working with one's hands.

The K-Shaped Stock Market

Just as the economy is K-shaped, so too is the stock market.

It would only be a slight oversimplification to say that AI-related stocks are thriving while everything else is surviving (at best) or languishing (at worst).

A big reason for this is that big tech companies are deploying their massive war chests of cash into the AI buildout, which has ripple effects outward to semiconductor stocks, data center developers and landlords, independent power producers, and so on.

Rather shockingly, AI companies are pumping about 130% of their EBITDA into capex, while non-AI companies are spending about 50% of EBITDA on capex.

Talk about K-shaped!

Everyone involved in AI seems to be winning right now. Whether you're the gold miner or the guy selling picks and shovels, you're winning. (At least for now.)

The top 10 stocks in the S&P 500 (SPY) (save, perhaps, the 10th largest holding, Berkshire Hathaway) all have various irons in the AI fire, some more than others.

At this point, these top 10 mega-cap giants account for about one-third of total SPY earnings and ~40% of the SPY's total market cap.

The top 20 stocks, for what it's worth, account for more than 50% of SPY's market cap.

At risk of belaboring the point, that means that the bottom 480 stocks in the index account for less than half of its total market cap.

The performance of major stock market indices has rarely been more divergent from that of the broader economy than it is today.

The following is a chart that illustrates this within the stock market.

The Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT) is populated exclusively by AI-related companies, while the larger Nasdaq Index (QQQ) is mostly but not entirely technology and AI-related companies. Meanwhile, for scale, I show the heavily value-oriented Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), which have gone precisely nowhere over the last year.

Again, talk about K-shaped!

Averages have become incredibly deceptive in both the economy and stock market. Within those averages are contained both the thriving affluent and the languishing paycheck-to-paycheck class, both the thriving AI-related stocks and the languishing value-oriented stocks, both the cash-rich mega-caps and the debt-burdened small caps.

My Dividend Growth Buy List Today

It's a sobering thought for a value-oriented dividend growth investor like me that, whatever happens in the stock market in the near term (whether big tech pulls back, goes higher, or stays rangebound), the trends fueling the K-shaped economy and market may very well endure.

As population growth declines, Boomers continue retiring from the workforce, AI swallows up entry-level jobs, and tariffs whittle away purchasing power, it seems likely that these K-shaped patterns aren't going away anytime soon.

For now, it's enough to give me pause and spur contemplation but not enough to make me alter my investment strategy.

There are still some value-oriented yet GARP-y dividend growers I find attractive and buyable.

My "buy list" includes six of them:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Tower (AMT) 3.4% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.4% High-Single-Digit
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Iron Mountain (IRM) 3.4% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
Permian Resources (PR) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Public Storage (PSA) 4.3% Mid-Single-Digit

One's long-term success as a dividend growth investor depends a lot on how accurately one can project forward dividend growth. I try to be fairly conservative in this department.

If you aim to begin withdrawing income from your portfolio in 10 years, it would be better to buy a 4%-yielding stock growing at 5% per year than a 5%-yielding stock growing at 2.5% per year or a 6% yield that doesn't grow at all.

Many of my readers are withdrawers or soon-to-be withdrawers who are trying to maximize current income that hopefully grows fast enough to beat inflation. I don't blame you!

But I'm a DGIer in my 30s trying to optimize my portfolio for maximum income 10-plus years from now.

That's why, if I'm buying something just for the income, I like to get at least a 6.5% yield, preferably 7% or higher.

Whereas, if I'm buying a dividend growth stock, I want to reasonably project a 10-year yield-on-cost of at least 6.5% (preferably over 7%).

Of course, it's very hard to forecast a 10-year YoC accurately, but my assumption is that if I get even decently close, it should be a successful DGI investment. "Shoot for the moon and land among the stars," as the saying goes.

Most of these stocks were on my buy list last week, so see that article for my still-relevant comments on five of the six names.

The only newbie is Permian Resources, a pure-play Permian Basin oil and gas producer.

I've been very impressed by the smart capital allocation of PR's young gun co-CEOs Will Hickey and James Walter. This year, they've managed to repurchase $43 million of stock (at very low prices), make multiple bolt-on acquisitions in the Permian, and earn PR's first investment grade credit rating (BBB-) from Fitch, all while keeping debt low at 1.0x net debt to EBITDA.

To be fair, short of some geopolitical event that causes oil and gas prices to spike, PR lacks any obvious catalyst to send its share price higher. But as long as the price of oil stays more or less rangebound going forward, the company should have plenty of cash flow to continue the spree of smart capital allocation.

PR's soaring post-COVID rally may be over, but now it's time for the dividend growth story to begin.

data.title: QQQI: Double-Digit Yield With Big Tech Power
data.description:
The NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) is one of my favorite ETFs to buy right now. Combining monthly dividends, big tech exposure, tax efficient returns, and an actively managed options strategy that returns high yields and also has upside potential. QQQI has done better than the S&P 500 and has kept up with the Nasdaq in a roaring bull market. If QQQI can deliver this level of return in a market where its strategy should be at a disadvantage, the alpha in a more volatile, range-bound environment is significant.

QQQI has not been around for long, and so it is difficult to see how it would perform in different market conditions. We have had 2 years in a row where the markets have returned more than 20%, with 2023 being 24.23% and 2024 being 23.31%. As of August 14th, SPY is up 9.5% YTD. These returns are out of the ordinary, and way above the S&P 500's historical average of about 11%. I am not saying that we are going to get a crash, or even saying that this trend cannot continue, but QQQI is one of the strategies I am using to protect myself against a potential slowdown towards the end of the year or a red year in 2026 or 2027. Either way, if QQQI can capture around 98% of QQQ's return with its covered call strategy, it should heavily outperform the underlying in a sideways market or a bear market, where a covered call strategy shines. For me, I see QQQI as a no brainer way to go long the Nasdaq in a time when there could be uncertainty and volatility in the market.

High Income ETFs

A high-income ETF is an exchange traded fund structured to produce substantial, regular payouts by holding an underlying stock or index and selling options against it, typically through a covered call strategy. These ETFs have gained massive popularity over the past years, with more and more options coming out monthly. This competition is great for us consumers, but not all high-income ETFs are built the same. There are a lot of really high-yielding ones, and even ones that pay weekly, but the reality is that a lot of these will greatly underperform their benchmarks. Investors can suffer huge NAV (net asset value) erosion and inconsistent and unstable dividends.

In my books, what makes a great high-income ETF is the following:

1. Generates sustainable and consistent monthly income

2. No NAV erosion

3. Be able to capture some upside in bull markets

*Bonus point: Tax efficiency

As you may have guessed, QQQI checks every box.

QQQI's Options Strategy

QQQI usually uses 2 strategies to generate its monthly income + some upside if the market runs.

Selling Covered calls: QQQI primarily sells out of the money [OTM], usually 1.5-4% away from the current price. These are typically 6-7 weeks from expiration.

AS QQQI is a dynamic, actively managed ETF, it has prediction models and will typically only sell calls on 70%-80% of the shares it owns to capture some upside. But as this is dynamic, we can see the fund selling calls on up to 95% of shares or even as low as 60% depending on market conditions.

Buying Calls: Buying long calls when volatility is compressed to participate in upside. When volatility is low (VXN below 12), covered call premiums become much lower, so for the fund to meet income demand, it has to sell calls on 95%-100% of its shares. Buying long calls at this point would be used as an insurance policy to make sure that if the stock runs while they are heavily covered, they can have some upside appreciation.

NAV Erosion

NAV erosion, or Net Asset Value erosion, is when the value of an investment fund’s shares gradually decreases over time, even if the market stays flat or goes up a little. This can happen if the fund gives out more money to investors than it earns from its investments and options strategy. Essentially, the fund is dipping into its own capital to make those payments, which shrinks the overall value of the fund’s assets. This has not happened with QQQI, and I don't think this should be a concern. With its dynamic options strategy and great management, I have full confidence that QQQI will not suffer NAV decay.

Tax Efficiency

The IRS has specific types of "contracts" that get special tax treatment. One of these contracts is index options, as the ones we use on QQQI. These are called Section 1256 contracts. These contracts allow our profits to be taxed 60% as long-term capital gains, and 40% as short-term capital gains. This is a major win as long-term capital gains are taxed at a way lower rate than short-term, and these rules apply even if you held the contract for 1 day.

Additionally, much of the payout capital returned so far has been labeled as return on capital [ROC], which is not taxed immediately, and instead lowers your cost basis and is only taxed when the shares are sold or your cost basis becomes 0.

Breaking down QQQI:

  • Inception: January 30, 2024
  • AUM: ~$3.7 billion
  • Expense Ratio: 0.68%
  • Underlying Index: Nasdaq-100 (NDX)
  • Distribution: Monthly, much of it classified as return of capital [ROC]
  • Issuer: NEOS Investments
  • Notable Recognition: Winner of the Best New Active ETF at the 2025 ETF.com awards

QQQI vs. QQQ - Total Returns

Over the past year, QQQI has returned 27.46% compared to 29.23% for QQQ - an exceptionally narrow gap for a covered call ETF. This level of upside capture is impressive, especially as we are in a raging bull market, where covered call strategies typically lag further behind due to the upside cap from option writing. Historically, these funds do better in choppy or sideways markets, yet QQQI has managed to stay even with its underlying index while still delivering a double-digit yield. This speaks to the effectiveness of its actively managed options strategy, which balances income generation with meaningful participation in market rallies. All of these wonderful gains have much less volatility than the underlying, with Beta being about 1 on QQQI.

QQQI vs SPY - Total Returns

Over the past year, QQQI has outpaced the S&P 500 by more than 5%, and it’s done so in the middle of a bull market, a scenario where covered call ETFs typically lag rather than lead.

Peers

QQQI should be in a league of its own, it has heavily outperformed its peers.

ETF Yield Coverage 1 Yr returns NAV Trend Strategy Tax Efficiency
QQQI ~14.5% Dynamic (60–95%) 24.23% Stable Active Yes (1256 + ROC)
QYLD ~11% 100% Static 10.23% Declining Passive No
JEPQ ~10% ~80% Static 18.89% Stable Active No


This chart really shows the difference in options strategy taking place, QYLD for example, uses a completely static options strategy and is lagging massively behind its competitors. It only captures 60% of QQQ's gains, while QQQI will over 98%. JEPQ does perform better than QYLD, but, its static strategy still does not come close to QQQI's performance. We should also mention that QQQI also has the extra bonus of being tax-friendly, so not only are the returns better, but that is an extra perk.

Risks

Market risk - As QQQI is heavily exposed to large cap tech companies and growth stocks, if we see a rotation out of big tech, it will hurt QQQI. While some covered call protection is there, it would still suffer and bleed with the underlying.

Capped upside - If we see the underlying QQQ go up even faster than we are seeing right now, we may see a bigger difference in performance between QQQI and QQQ, as the covered calls do limit the upside.

Distribution Variability – Although payouts have been relatively stable so far, option premium income can fluctuate with volatility, which could cause occasional changes in distribution amounts. So if you are dependent solely on QQQI income to survive, I would recommend diversifying into other investments as well.

Limited Track Record - QQQI was launched on January 30, 2024, so it has less than two years of performance history. It is hard to predict how this fund will perform in different market conditions. Although it has recovered very well from the tariff lows, and also done very well during the "V" shaped recovery to all time highs on the markets. Past results don't guarantee future success, and we will just have to monitor closely.

Active Management Risk - The strategy relies on NEOS’s managers making smart choices. Poor timing or decisions could hurt returns or income. I believe they have algorithms and the best and most sophisticated decision-making tools, but it is still an actively managed fund, and we should take note of that risk.

Conclusion

QQQI has proven itself to be one of the best high-income ETFs on the market, and it checks all the boxes: sustainable high monthly income, no NAV erosion, meaningful upside capture, and bonus points for tax efficiency.

In just a year, it has meaningfully outperformed the S&P 500, and nearly matched QQQ's gains in a bull market, and done so with lower volatility. Its dynamic options strategy allows it to adapt to different market conditions and get the best of both worlds in income and growth.

I believe QQQI is great for investors who want to go long the Nasdaq, but also want some cushion in case we get a slowdown or a market pullback without sacrificing much of the upside at all. QQQI earns my Strong Buy rating and remains an ETF I'm personally buying every day.

data.title: THQ: 13.3% Top Contrarian Healthcare Yield, Discounted Price
data.description:
If you like big-yield opportunities, trading at discounted prices, the healthcare sector CEF (closed-end fund) I review in this report is worth considering. It currently offers a 13.3% yield (paid monthly) and trades at a big contrarian discount (its top holdings are down big, and it trades at a 9% discount to net asset value). This report takes a closer look at what is happening here (in terms of fund strategy, valuation and risks) and then concludes with my strong opinion on who might want to invest.

About abrdn Healthcare Opportunities Fund (NYSE:THQ)

According to the Aberdeen website, THQ: “seeks current income and long-term capital appreciation,” and it does so by owning primarily healthcare stocks (recently ~74%) as well as corporate bonds in the healthcare sector (recently ~14%) and small allocations to healthcare REITs, venture capital and derivatives (~5%, ~5% and ~2%, respectively).

And it achieves these allocations with the help of leverage (or borrowed money) which can magnify returns and income in the good times, but magnify risks in the bad times (more on this later).

And speaking of “bad times,” the fund has recently performed terribly as nearly all of its top holdings are underperforming the S&P 500 (some of them by a lot) as you can see in the following chart.

(LLY) (UNH) (ABT) (ABBV) (MRK)

Worth mentioning, THQ's leverage ratio was recently 23.8% (a lot for a primarily equity fund, but still reasonable considering the healthcare sector is historically less volatile, and with a lower “beta,” than the S&P 500, and say technology stocks in particular, and the fund has a significant allocation to bonds too—also lower volatility).

What is a Closed-End Fund? (CEF)

Before getting into more details on THQ, it’s worth briefly reviewing what a CEF is (because that’s what THQ is). A closed-end fund is a basket of investments (THQ recently had 115 individual positions) thereby providing some instant diversification; and unlike open-end mutual funds and exchange-traded funds, CEFs typically start with a fixed number of shares and trade based on supply and demand (i.e. there is no mechanism in place to ensure the market price stays close to the net asset value (NAV) of the underlying holdings, as is the case for open-end mutual funds and ETFs) and thereby CEFs can trade at wide discounts and premiums to NAV thereby creating significant risks and opportunities (more on this later).

For reference, here is a look at 7 big-yield closed-end fund questions I always ask myself before investing, and I will refer to this graphic throughout this report.

Why has THQ performance been so bad?

As you can see in the earlier chart, THQ performance has been weak over the last year (down -18%) as compared to the S&P 500 (up +21%), and the main reason is because the big healthcare stocks it owns (e.g. Eli Lilly (LLY) and UnitedHealth Group (UNH), the largest holdings) have been terrible (and the whole healthcare sector is down too).

Additionally, THQ is trading at an increased discount to NAV (which has been an additional drag on price performance) as “fearful investors” have put selling pressure on the shares (particularly in recent trading sessions, see chart below).

Further still, the fund’s unique custom benchmark (80% S&P Composite 1500 Healthcare Index, 15% S&P 500 HealthCare Corporate Bond Index, 5% S&P Composite 1500 HealthCare REITs Index) has also been relatively weak. In a nutshell, the healthcare sector has NOT done well recently.

Is THQ an attractive contrarian opportunity?

The answer to this question depends on your personal goals; however, it is notable that healthcare stocks can be a bit of a defensive play versus the more volatile S&P 500, and many investors believe the top holdings (such as Lilly and UNH) are attractive (Morningstar rates them 3 and 4 stars, respectively, and suggests they have 2.3% and 36% immediate upside, respectively).

Further still, the discounted price versus NAV (from fearful selling pressure) arguably makes the shares significantly more attractive than they were. However, it still depends on your personal situation, goals and investment philosophy (more on this in the conclusion).

Risks Factors:

Of course, THQ faces a variety of risk factors investors need to consider. For example:

  • Leverage: As mentioned, THQ uses leverage (borrowed money) to magnify returns and income, but this also magnifies risks in the bad times (when it sells off, it can sell off hard, like it just did).
  • Fees and expenses are another risk. For example, the interest rate on borrowed money (leverage) adds to the fund’s total expense ratio (as you can see in the graphic below), and the overall expense ratio (recently 3.28%) is very expensive compared to just owning individual stocks on your own (basically free) or just buying a healthcare sector ETF (XLV) which has an expense ratio of only 0.08%, and then creating your own distributions (by selling some of your shares).

  • New Management: Aberdeen (now abrdn) took over management of THQ from Tekla in October of 2023, and the transition creates some risk. For example, the new management company was quick to raise the distribution after the acquisition (see chart below), but this also increases the risks.

  • Is the distribution sustainable? The increased distribution calls into question whether the distribution is sustainable over the long-term. For example, as you can see in the table below, THQ has been using some ROC (return of capital) to support the distribution recently. A little ROC is fine from time to time, but if it is continued for the long term it eats away at the net asset value, reduces the future earnings power (and income power) of the fund and makes the distribution increasingly at risk of requiring a reduction. However, even if abrdn is forced to reduce the distribution, it will still likely be quite large and this will also increase the future health of the fund.

Also noteworthy, short-term capital gains have been used recently to help support the distribution, and these short-term gains may be taxed at a higher rate for you than long-term gains (depending on your tax bracket, and whether you own THQ in a taxable account or non-taxable retirement account).

  • The discount can get bigger. We’ve already considered the recently increased THQ share price discount versus NAV to be attractive (all else equal, I prefer to purchase attractive CEFs at a discount to NAV, not a premium). However, that doesn’t mean the discount cannot still get bigger (which would hurt the value of your position), considering it has been bigger throughout history as you can see in the earlier “Premium/Discount” chart.
  • Is the healthcare sector right for you? Rather than concentrating your investments in the healthcare sector (like THQ does) some investors prefer to invest more broadly across all sectors (such as an S&P 500 ETF). However, others prefer the perceived safety (and historical safety) of the healthcare sector (especially when it has been underperforming and presents a contrarian opportunity) particularly when combined with THQ’s big steady monthly distribution payments to investors (i.e. put it on “autopilot” and just let the distribution cash roll in).

The Bottom Line

THQ is absolutely NOT for everyone. However, if you are an income-focused investor that likes to purchase big distributions when they trade at a discounted price (i.e. the discount to NAV has just increased based on fearful selling, and the healthcare sector may be due for a bit of a contrarian rebound), THQ is worth considering. I currently own shares in my High Income NOW portfolio.

data.title: Powell Industries: New All-Time Highs Are Likely On The Horizon
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Powell Industries (NASDAQ:POWL), I upgraded my rating to a “buy,” with a price target of $233 per share, as I believed that we would start to see an uptick in new orders and backlog with the capacity expansion at its Houston facility. This led me to believe that its forward consensus estimates were likely too conservative and were going to be rerated higher.

Since then, the stock is up close to 36%, outperforming the S&P 500 by close to 6x, as can be seen below.

Powell Industries recently reported their Q3 FY25 earnings, where, despite a revenue miss, its Electric Utility and Commercial segments continued to grow fast and contributed to a higher share of Total Revenue.

Meanwhile, both new orders and backlog started ticking higher both on a year-over-year and sequential basis, like I had originally predicted, as the company booked large projects in its Oil & Gas and Electric Utility markets and expects 65% of its backlog to convert into revenue in the next twelve months.

Plus, management is optimistic on the outlook for all its end markets, where it particularly looks to strengthen its Electric Utility market with the agreement to acquire Remsdaq Limited, which is an established name for electrical automation in the regulated utility market in the UK.

With earnings per share estimates getting revised significantly higher since the time of my last writing, I believe that the stock has a 50% potential upside from its current levels, which could set it up for new all-time highs.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating with a renewed price target of $381 per share.

Revenue missed, but Electric Utility and Commercial grew while margins expanded.

Powell Industries reported their Q3 FY25 earnings recently, where revenues declined 1% YoY to $286M, missing consensus estimates by over 5%.

Out of the $286M in revenue, its Oil & Gas and Petrochemical segment declined at a faster rate compared to the previous quarter at 17% YoY. However, the weakness was offset by strength in the company’s Electric Utility segment, which grew over 31% YoY, while its Commercial segment also grew close to 17% YoY.

Note that on a year-over-year basis, the revenue contribution from Powell Industries’ Electricity Utility and Commercial segments is continuing to rise significantly from a combined 34% in Q3 last year to 44% in the latest quarter.

Plus, despite the revenue miss for the quarter, Powell Industries continues to demonstrate superior operational discipline, with both its gross and operating margins expanding by 240 basis points and 110 basis points, respectively, to 30.7% and 21%, respectively, as a result of efficient project execution and closeouts, with margin rates exiting backlog benefiting from large projects nearing completion.

New orders and backlog picked up; acquisition of Remsdaq strengthens its electric utility business

In my previous post, I had discussed that one of the reasons for Powell Industries underperforming the broader market index at the time of my writing was tied to the softness in new orders and backlog.

However, I had explained that we should see a pickup in both new orders and backlog in the coming quarters, as they had just received occupancy permits on the capacity expansion at their electrical products facility in Houston.

Indeed, in Q3, Powell Industries saw an uptick in new orders on both a year-over-year and sequential basis to $362M, as they booked two large orders for custom power control rooms in the Oil & Gas market, which have a combined total of over $80M. Simultaneously, the company also booked a $60M order in the Electric Utility market for a new power generation facility, which the management pointed out was a testament to their strategic efforts to diversify their business and establish themselves as a trusted partner to their utility customers, with half of new orders in the Electric Utility market.

Meanwhile, backlog also grew both on a year-over-year and sequential basis to $1.4B during the quarter, with 65% of backlog expected to convert into revenue over the next 12 months.

With management optimistic on the outlook for each of their end markets, Powell Industries also announced an agreement to acquire Remsdaq Limited, which is an established name for electrical automation in the regulated utility market in the UK. Up until now, the company would be able to influence the hardware side of their electrical automation solutions roughly 30% of the time. With the addition of Remsdaq, Powell Industries will be able to strengthen its electrical automation platform by offering a 100% Powell-built solution to the utility market.

Simultaneously, the company is also expanding its product initiatives to build on its Commercial momentum as they pursue further revenue diversification with the design of a low-voltage switchgear product targeted at data centers. During the earnings call, the management pointed out that they continue to see “broad and increased activity” within the data center market and are confident that they will be able to penetrate this market with new products.

Forward earnings revised significantly higher, pointing to a 50% potential upside.

Despite the stock rising close to 36% since the time of my writing, outperforming the S&P 500 by roughly 6x, the company continues to see its forward revenue estimates getting revised lower, as can be seen below.

This could be due to the uncertain tariff environment, which could impact materials like steel and batteries used in substations, along with potential volatility in the data center market.

However, its forward earnings estimates have been revised higher quite significantly over the last month, which I believe is quite optimistic.

Coming to Powell Industries' price target, this is how I would approach it.

In my previous post, I had said that Powell Industries was trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 13.4, with its expected earnings for FY25 projected to grow 7.4% YoY at that time. But, I believed that there was room for its forward price-to-earnings multiple to expand to 17 when taking the S&P 500 as a proxy.

Since then, its forward PE has indeed expanded to slightly over 17, which brings us to the question of whether there is any further upside left.

With the revised earnings estimates, Powell Industries is now expected to grow its earnings per share by 17.25%.

This is roughly 1.9 times the expected earnings per share growth rate of the S&P 500 for FY25.

Therefore, taking the 10-year average PE of the S&P 500 of 18 as an anchor, I believe it is possible for Powell to see its forward price-to-earnings multiple expand to 27, which represents an upside of 50% from its current levels, translating to a price target of $381 per share.

My final verdict and conclusions

I believe Powell Industries is back on track, with new orders and backlog ticking higher, while it looks to strengthen its positioning in the Electric Utility market with the acquisition of Remsdaq Limited, while introducing new products to further penetrate its Commercial market, which houses data centers as well.

With earnings estimates revised significantly higher since the time of my last writing, I will reiterate my “buy” rating with a renewed price target of $381 per share.

data.title: Value Line Grows Its Asset Management Services As Demand For Research Wanes
data.description:
Few financial services companies have a history like Value Line (NASDAQ:VALU). Its signature publication, the Value Line Investment Survey, has been produced for almost 100 years and is widely recognized as one of the best sources of stock research in the world. It provides comprehensive data for approximately 1700 of the most actively traded stocks and is updated on a weekly basis. Its analysis and predictive metrics are considered some of the most accurate in the industry.

Unfortunately for Value Line, its traditional core product has been losing its prestige in the marketplace as financial information has become democratized and fewer people are willing to pay its subscription fee. From Morningstar to the Motley Fool and even Seeking Alpha, there are simply more ways for investors to get much of the same information, often for a lower price. Value Line has tried to adjust its old school print distribution methods by offering digital content. It has also begun leveraging its expertise to serve as an asset manager for stock and bond mutual funds.

On July 29, the company released its results from FY 2025, and despite the slowing demand for its publications, earnings are growing at a modest pace. The company has also been increasing dividends consistently and buying back its stock in an effort to return more of its profits to shareholders. The stock chart from this year, however, shows that the overall market is not impressed. VALU has lost over 27% since January 1, even as the overall market has reached new record highs.

After looking through the company’s recent annual report, I can see the positive results from the company’s shift toward digital content and fund management. However, these improvements are being weighed down by continued declines in overall publication revenue. The last 11 articles written about Value Line on Seeking Alpha have given its stock a Hold rating, and my analysis will make it an even dozen. However, the company’s growth in asset management and increased efforts to return cash to shareholders are certainly worth following in the year ahead.

Company Profile

Founded in 1931 by Arnold Bernhard, Value Line is a financial services and investment research firm. The company refers to itself as an “authority in reliable and unbiased information”, providing research for individual companies and industries as well as the entire market and US economy. Data provided by Value Line is widely used by investment professionals as well as individual investors for consistent insight on stocks, ETFs, and mutual funds.

Value Line has a market cap of $366.8 million. Almost 92% of the company is owned by Arnold Bernhard & Company. (Bernhard passed away in 1987.) Because of this, VALU has a relatively low average trading volume. In July, there were five days where less than 1000 shares of the stock exchanged hands. The portion of Value Line that is not controlled by AB & Co. continues to shrink as the company is buying back shares on a regular basis. During FY 2025, the company bought 11,480 shares bringing the total purchased under its current buyback plan from 2022 to 47,202. This may not seem significant, but there were only 9,409,522 total shares of the stock outstanding at the end of FY 2025, and only about 8% of those, are not held by AB & Co.

Revenue for the company has been in a decline for four straight years as the demand for the company’s “old school” print materials has decreased. In FY 2022, the company reported revenue of $40.5 million. By FY 2025, the number was only $35.1 million. Value Line has been able to make up for some of this drop off by investing in more digital content and expanding its product lines to include services through EULAV Asset Management. Value Line holds non-voting revenues and profits interests in EULAV, which serves as the investment advisor to its mutual funds and portfolios.

Thanks in large part to its increased income from EULAV, Value Line has been able to keep its bottom line growing for three consecutive years, and Value Line has committed to returning more of its profits to shareholders through increasing dividends. In April of 2025, the company announced that it was raising its quarterly payout from $0.30 to $0.325, giving the stock an annual dividend of $1.30. This marks the 11th straight year that VALU has increased its dividend. The stock currently yields 3.34%. The five-year average growth rate for its dividend increases is almost 9%.

Analysis of Value Line

Value Line reported FY 2025 earnings of $2.20 per share with total net income of $20.7 million. These totals were 8.8% ahead of FY 2024 and 14.4% ahead of FY 2023. The company’s income is derived from one of three sources – publications and research (both print and online), receipts from its interests in EULAV, and gains from its own investment portfolio. While the publication and research side is on a multi-year decline, the other two sources are growing. Income from EULAV totaled $18.3 million, an increase of $5.0 million or 37.9% from FY 2024. Investment gains of $3.2 million were an improvement of $474,000, or 17.1%, when compared to FY 2024. Trends for each of the sources mentioned here for the last three years can be seen in the snapshot below which was taken from VALU's 2025 10-K.

As can be seen, the company’s income from EULAV Asset Management has been increasing at about the same rate as the income from VALU’s traditional operations has been declining. The good news for Value Line shareholders is that while those two income streams were about equal in 2023, the income from EULAV is now over three times as large as VALU’s income from operations. The company’s investment gains equaled over half of the income from operations in FY 2025 after being only about 10.2% in FY 2023.

Further detail from the most recent annual report shows that not only are total revenues down from print and digital publications, but the company is having to rely more on new sales just to keep it at current levels. Renewals represented 89% of sales in 2023. While renewal rates were slightly higher in 2025 for digital sales, print sales renewals dropped to 85.9%.

Below is further detail about the drop in top-line numbers from the company’s traditional revenue sources. Print revenue is falling faster than digital, but the company is reporting that this core business is still holding on to some pricing power with its customer base. For the last three years, Value Line has reported that sales of its higher-price, higher margin publications have been stronger than sales of “starter products” that have a lower price. In addition to income from print and digital publications, the company also records revenues from copyright fees, and those have fallen by low double-digit percentages in 2025 and 2024 with the most recent total being $10.4 million. According to the company, copyright fees depend on the assets under management in financial products that pay to use Value Line proprietary information. These fees rise and fall based on interest rates and ratings from fund rating agencies.

The last piece of the puzzle for Value Line is the size of its assets under management through EULAV. The income from these funds has been increasing, but most of the recent increase in the total portfolio can be attributed to market value changes, not a significant rush of inflows. In FY 2024, the equity fund assets increased by 35.6% with inflows outpacing redemptions by 60.8%. In FY 2025, inflows were $163.3 million, or just 16.0% ahead of outflows, even as the total AUM for the company grew by just over $500 million.

Value Line has been able to keep expenses relatively consistent over the last three years. Salaries and office administration fees actually decreased in FY 2025. The company spent a noticeably higher amount on advertising and promotion in its last fiscal year.

One bright spot for Value Line is its clean balance sheet. The company has virtually no debt. Value Line reported $77.4 million in cash and short-term securities at the end of FY 2025. That total included $33.6 million in cash in various banks and money market funds, giving shareholders about $3.60 in cash per share of VALU stock. If you back out the cash from the stock’s current price, the company is trading at an adjusted PE of 15.9 instead of 17.6.

My final decision on how to rate the stock came down to the fact that Value Line's best days ahead clearly rely on its asset management business. The company’s traditional business of selling research data to individual and institutional investors is declining, and the likelihood of that changing in the future is slim. If we ignore the negatives of the research business, the company is really just a asset manager like Janus Henderson (JHG), Franklin Resources (BEN), or Invesco (IVZ). Those stocks currently trade at PE multiples of 12.2, 12.4 and 12.0 respectively, making VALU look slightly overvalued at the current time, despite its stock price decline this year.

Risks to Consider

As mentioned in my introduction, Value Line is trying to compete in an industry that has faced a great deal of disruption from free and lower-cost providers of investment research. Competition can cause pricing pressure, lower subscription interest, and an overall decline in market share. The funds offered by EULAV may face outflows if the returns on their funds do not match the returns from other asset managers who often have lower fees and/ or higher marketing budgets to attract new investors.

The revenue generated by the assets under management for EULAV is also subject to declines caused by adverse market conditions, including but not limited to rising inflation, rising unemployment, or a decrease in GDP that could lead to a recession in the US. Prolonged poor returns from assets managed by EULAV could also lead to redemptions by investors, which could decrease the income due to Value Line.

As of the end of FY 2025, Value Line reported additional investment assets of $43.3 million, not including its interests in EULAV. Included in this $43.3 million were shares of several US equity ETFs, including the SPDR Series Trust S&P Dividend ETF (SDY), ProShares Trust S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL), and iShares DJ Select Dividend ETF (DVY). The company also reported ownership of government bonds and bank certificates of deposits. All of these ETFs are subject to losses that would be connected to a decline in the overall stock market. The bonds and CDs are subject to declines in income that would be related to lower US interest rates.

There is one other point to consider for anyone who would consider buying shares in VALU. The average trading volume in the stock over the last three months is about 4300 shares, and there were five days in July where the daily volume was under 1000. Limit orders are highly recommended for any purchase to ensure the purchase price does not move dramatically when an order is placed.

The Bottom Line

I read through my research several times before I landed on my final decision to give the stock a Hold rating. VALU has traded in a relatively narrow range since February of this year. With earnings growth in the range of just 6 to 9% in the last two years, I do not feel like the potential rewards for stock ownership here outweigh the risks.

data.title: 3 Super Popular Stocks That Still Have A Lot Of Room To Run
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this month, I wrote an article titled "3 Super Popular Stocks I Wouldn't Touch With A 10-Foot Pole," where I discussed a number of companies that I consider to be value traps for long-term (dividend) investors.

Two of these companies were already performing poorly, which led many to ask the question of whether they should jump in. In that article, I explained why they shouldn't.

Well, in this article, I'm doing the exact opposite.

I will discuss a number of high-flying popular companies that I expect to continue doing well, despite the market's lofty valuation and the (fair) assumption that a lot of good news may already have been priced in.

But before I do that, it's time we discuss the Big Picture, which includes a few charts today that truly fascinate me.

So, let's keep this intro short for once and get right to it!

A "Muddy Waters" Market

If there's one thing we have discussed a lot, it's the market's unfavorable valuation. Going into this month, the market traded at more than 22.0x forward earnings, the highest number since the early 2000s, if we exclude the pandemic, when analyst expectations were initially all over the place.

As obvious as it may sound, the higher the market's valuation, the worse the longer-term risk/reward, as the data below shows quite well. This makes sense. When the market has high expectations, there's less room for positive surprises and a greater potential for disappointment if those expectations are not met.

Moreover, there's a lot more evidence that suggests the risk/reward is unfavorable, including Bank of America's fund manager survey, which shows that average cash levels are close to 4.0%, one of the lowest levels over the past twenty years, and roughly a full point below the long-term average.

But wait, there's more!

More than 90% of surveyed fund managers say the market is overvalued in the United States, a number that has consistently been elevated since the pandemic, as the chart below displays quite well.

And that's still not everything.

More than a fifth of fund managers see a "no landing" scenario, meaning the Fed is expected to defeat inflation without creating a recession. Just 5% expect a hard landing, one of the lowest levels in recent years.

In other words, looking at these charts, it's almost fair to say that "everything" is going well, which is kind of ironic, as cyclic growth is in the toilet (for lack of a more sophisticated description), and the economy largely depends on aggressive AI spending, as I discussed in a recent article.

However, that's exactly the point of this market. While people make fun of the saying "This time is different," it's truly an accurate statement, as not a single stock market environment has occurred twice, ever.

Technically speaking, there are a number of reasons why a high P/E ratio can be justified, including low-interest rates (currently not the case), no alternatives (currently not the case, as bonds are yielding more than 4%), and exceptional companies (currently the case).

As we can see below, while the S&P 500 is expensive compared to its 20-year average, the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 is even more expensive.

We all know that the Nasdaq is home to the biggest technology stocks, which also dominate the S&P 500, as the market's biggest ten stocks account for nearly 40% of its weighting, a blowout number by any comparison.

While this creates concentration risks, it's currently the one big reason why this market seems to have legs, as investors are basically buying a basket that "entirely" (I'm exaggerating, but you get my point) on a small basket of exceptional companies that all have ultra wide moats, strong innovation, superb balance sheets, and a global reach.

Even better, if we believe AI is indeed our modern version of the Industrial Revolution, we can assume the impact on profit margins will be huge.

AI as a 4.0 industrial revolution is increasingly bringing smart solutions to humans. AI brings benefits in terms of reduced energy consumption, induced cost-effectiveness, reduced operational risks, and increased performance matrices.

[...]

Resulting in reduced energy consumption, enhanced cost-effectiveness, mitigated operational risks, and improved performance metrics. - Malik et al. (2024)

Right now, this is a huge factor, as the fascinating chart from (X/itsatrap2025) shows below. It adjusts the P/E ratio for the market's profit margin. Until the early 2000s, there was little difference between the two lines, as the American economy was very much focused on "hardware," including industrial stocks.

Since then, we have seen a bigger divergence, which has grown substantially in recent years, courtesy of the AI revolution and other profitability tailwinds.

So, does this mean everything I have ever written about the market's valuation was wrong?

No, absolutely not. And I'm not saying that to protect my ego.

  • The situation is still crystal clear. This market requires perfection. There is no room for S&P 500 companies to report underwhelming margin growth without the risk of a market correction.
  • AI adoption and a "higher for longer" interest rate still prefer a trickle-down effect where market strength broadens.
  • The companies that are currently driving the AI revolution are increasingly competing with each other, which makes it way more likely that the market's biggest 10 holdings will look very different ten years from now.

That's why I am so picky when it comes to this market. If stocks were to drop 20-25% in the weeks ahead, buying stocks would be much easier. Now, it's a challenge.

Luckily, there's still a lot of good stuff out there, even among companies that are already on everyone's radar.

That's what the next part of this article is about.

Three Popular Stocks With Room To Run

#1 - Alphabet (GOOGL)

Alphabet is a company I have given a lot more attention to this year, including in an article written on March 30. Initially, the company came under a lot of pressure, as the market expected new AI language models to destroy the demand for Google Search, which is still the company's bread and butter.

However, these fears have not materialized.

As we can see below, in 2Q25, Google Services revenues rose by 12% to $82.5 billion, with Search revenue rising by 12% to $54.2 billion and YouTube ads up 13% to $9.8 billion.

Meanwhile, subscriptions, platforms, and devices saw 20% growth to $11.2 billion, which was driven by YouTube and Google One.

As a result, operating income came in at $33.1 billion with a 40.1% margin.

Even better, Google Cloud revenues increased by 32% to $13.6 billion, boosting operating income to $2.8 billion with a 20.7% margin, and a backlog of $106 billion. The backlog alone saw 38% year-over-year growth.

Moreover, the company is putting its large installed base to good use.

During its 2Q25 earnings call, it noted that usage growth has been truly remarkable, with monthly tokens processed doubling from 480 trillion in May to over 980 trillion.

The Gemini app, which competes with ChatGPT and others, now has more than 450 million monthly active users, with daily requests up over 50% compared to the first quarter.

Meanwhile, in Workspace, AI-powered features like meeting notes in Google Meet reached over 50 million users in June, and the new short video product Google Vids hit nearly 1 million monthly active users.

It's also quite interesting that YouTube is the number one streaming service in the United States, beating Disney+ by a huge margin since the start of 2025.

Additionally, the company is building a bigger footprint in the entire AI space, as it noted that its Veo 3, which is a highly successful video generation model, has gone viral, with more than 70 million videos generated since May. In fact, some of these videos are so realistic that a handful of them already fooled me.

Anyway, my point is that Alphabet continues to prove that AI isn't a risk but an opportunity. While I don't own it, I prefer it over almost all software-focused companies, as this giant is agile and capable of R&D spending that most cannot compete with.

Also, it trades at just 21.8x earnings, a great deal for a business expected to grow its EPS by 24%, 6%, and 14% in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively.

Conservatively speaking, without a higher multiple, the company has the potential to return more than 12.0% per year, which I consider conservative.

#2 - Amazon (AMZN)

Amazon is a company I recently brought up in an article titled "I Found A Dividend-Growth Combo That Could Beat The Market For Years."

Although Amazon doesn't pay a dividend, I paired it with Prologis (PLD), the world's largest industrial REIT, which owns many of Amazon's "mission-critical" warehouses that have provided it with a wide moat in global e-commerce.

When it comes to future growth, Amazon is a favorite of mine, as it has a similar installed base to Alphabet. While it doesn't have search engines, it has an installed base in e-commerce, which is boosted through measures like Prime, allowing the company to create a loyal customer base.

Then, there's Amazon Prime, Amazon Web Services, Project Kuiper, which provides dependable internet, and so many other services.

All of these areas are booming and providing the company with both a competitive edge and an opportunity to massively scale AI services.

For example, e-commerce sales in North America were up 11%. Net income in this area grew by nearly 50%. In this segment, it is moving into more premium and lower-cost goods with same-day delivery, competing with companies like Walmart.

Although its stock price didn't like somewhat conservative guidance and the fact that Amazon Web Services is reporting underwhelming growth compared to Google Cloud and Azure, it needs to be said that its much larger market share makes it harder to report competing year-over-year growth rates.

While Amazon isn't yet known for major AI breakthroughs, there's substantial progress on various fronts:

According to the company, its generative AI business is a multibillion-dollar segment growing at triple-digit percentages year-over-year, although it faces some supply constraints, which is normal in a market where innovation is held back by bottlenecks in power and infrastructure.

Key developments include the deployment of Trainium2, which is the company's custom AI chip that supports Anthropic's Claude models and Amazon Bedrock. AWS also introduced EC2 instances powered by NVIDIA Grace Blackwell Superchips, which offer high-performance computing for AI workloads.

This supports technologies like Alexa+, its satellite internet initiative, Project Kuiper, and so much more, making Amazon a company with the capability to disrupt its markets for a long time.

Moreover, the interesting thing is that analysts see a path to nearly $90 billion in free cash flow by 2027, roughly 4% of its market cap. That's despite an expected CapEx surge to $120 billion, more than twice its 2021 spending. - Article

Valuation-wise, Amazon trades at 35.4x earnings, which is justified by expectations of more than 20% EPS growth in 2025 and 2027, and roughly 15% growth in 2026, paving the way for 15-20% annual returns.

I also need to say that even e-commerce is growing rapidly in an environment of very poor consumer sentiment, which makes this even more impressive.

#3 - Visa (V)

Visa isn't an AI stock, but it shines when it comes to profitability, as just four stocks of the entire S&P 500 have a higher net income margin than Visa.

One of the reasons I wish I had bought Visa years ago is that it's essentially like buying a stake in global consumer spending. With a network of about 14,500 financial institutions, over 150 million merchant locations, 300 billion transactions, and 4.8 billion credentials, it benefits from the rise of cashless payments, international expansion, and growing middle classes in emerging markets where cashless payments are just starting to gain traction.

Despite consumer spending headwinds, the company is doing tremendously well, as its most recent earnings showed a net revenue increase of 14% to $10.2 billion.
