Earnings per share were up 23%, supported by higher margins and 8% growth in overall payments. Even cross-border payments were up 11%, despite the trade war and other spending-related headwinds.

Interestingly, the company's total credentials growth rate of 7% continues what is now a streak of nine consecutive quarters of at least 7% growth.

Moreover, the company is approaching 15 billion tokens, with more than 50% of e-commerce transactions now tokenized worldwide. Now, it's advancing toward the goal of 100% penetration. Essentially, this tokenization improves security and efficiency in digital transactions.

Or, to put it differently, the company uses tokens to protect sensitive information, making it much harder to break into the system.

In general, the company is using new tech to stay ahead, which obviously includes AI. For example, Via Intelligence Commerce allows for an AI-agent shopping experience, as it integrates APIs for AI-ready cards, including tokenization and authentication.

Then, developers can deploy these capabilities at scale. So far, more than 30 partners are testing it in what the company calls its "live sandbox."

It's also quite fascinating that "Tap to Everything" payments account for nearly 80% of all face-to-face transactions, which is interesting, as I often don't even bring my wallet anymore. I just pay with Apple Pay.

Moreover, with regard to the threat from stablecoins (crypto assets with a stable value), some investors are worried, as stablecoins could bypass Visa's traditional payment network by using the blockchain. By using stablecoins, large customers like Walmart could avoid Visa's transaction fees.

All they would need to do is launch a trusted stablecoin that customers can use to pay. It could massively hurt payment volumes.

However, Visa is working on that as well, as it helps banks issue stablecoins, with multichain support in various blockchains that already work with 25 fiat currencies. This way, the company is getting ahead of this risk and building a competitive moat that allows others to adopt payment alternatives.

With regard to its valuation, Visa trades at roughly 30x earnings, which is in line with its long-term average and justified by extremely resilient growth, a double-A credit rating, sky-high margins, and a great growth outlook.

Using FactSet data, analysts expect 12-15% annual EPS growth, a number I expect to remain resilient, as I see no risk of Visa being disrupted soon.

If I had more cash, I would love to buy Visa, as I believe it has what it takes to maintain double-digit annual total returns. Especially with AI, it should be able to further streamline its operations.

For now, it remains on my watchlist, next to Amazon and Alphabet.

Takeaway

This market is expensive. There's no sugarcoating it.

With sky-high valuations, low cash levels at hedge funds, and what seems like an almost unanimous belief that we are headed for a "no landing" scenario, it feels like the risk/reward is horrible.

And to a certain extent, that is correct.

But there's a twist, as this time is different. That's not because history won't repeat itself (it always does, in some form), but because the market's strength depends on a handful of exceptional companies that are driving the AI revolution, and have been on top of other secular trends as well.

That's why I'm being ultra-selective instead of turning my back on the market.

While a broad correction would (obviously) make buying easier, there are still a few gems worth owning, including businesses like Alphabet, Amazon, and Visa.

They combine wide moats, impressive innovation, and global dominance with valuations that leave room for more upside.

I don't own any of them due to my focus on dividend growers, and because I simply underestimated many of these companies, I would love to own at least 1-2 of these companies in the quarters ahead.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risks to these stocks, on top of general market correction risks, are innovation-related risks. Visa needs to stay on top of payment technologies, Alphabet needs to prove it can expand its footprint in the search business, and grow the AI segment so much that it creates an even wider moat.

Meanwhile, Amazon needs to show it can maintain leadership in AWS and use its installed base (i.e., e-commerce and Prime) to effectively implement AI.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday made solid gains for the week, helped by raised expectations for Federal Reserve interest rate cuts and hopes for progress on Russia and Ukraine.

The July consumer and producer inflation reports highlighted the week. The core consumer price index in July came in hotter than anticipated on a Y/Y basis, while the producer price index readings were significantly higher than expected. Both sets of data showed the effect of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on prices. After a weak July nonfarm payrolls reading earlier this month, market participants see this week's inflation data solidifying a September interest rate cut.

Developments between Russia and Ukraine also took center stage this week. U.S. President Trump and Russian leader Vladimir Putin convened in Alaska on Friday, and Trump later described the meeting as "very productive," even though no agreement was reached to resolve a war that has raged for nearly three and a half years.

Looking at the earnings calendar, Dow 30 component Cisco (CSCO) headlined the week. Also in focus were 13F filings—regulatory disclosures by major funds of their quarterly equity ownership changes. Berkshire Hathaway notably revealed a new stake in UnitedHealth (UNH).

For the week, the S&P (SP500) climbed +0.9%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) gained +0.8%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) added +1.7%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: Hope Bancorp: Over 5% Dividend Yield, But Stock Seems Fairly Valued
data.description:
Hope Bancorp, Inc. (NASDAQ:HOPE) reported a loss for the second quarter of 2025, partly due to one-time merger-related expenses following the acquisition of Territorial Bancorp. Further, the company’s earnings took a hit in the second quarter from the sale of low-yielding Available-for-Sale securities at a loss of $39 million, as mentioned in the earnings presentation. Going forward, the securities repositioning and acquisition will drive earnings. Hope Bancorp is currently offering a very attractive dividend yield, but the stock seems overvalued with the current market price significantly above the year-end target price.

Team Expansion to Counter Economic Slowdown in Main Markets

Hope Bancorp’s loan book jumped up by 8.3% in 2Q’25, mostly due to the acquisition of Territorial Bancorp. The target bank was based in Hawai’i, so the acquisition added yet another state to Hope Bancorp’s already diverse footprint. However, there is still significant geographical concentration, with more than three-quarters of the Commercial Real Estate portfolio being based in the states of California and New York.

The outlook for Hawaiian operations is slightly negative because of the condition of the tourism industry, which the Hawaiian economy relies on. Almost 20% of non-farm jobs in the state are in the leisure and hospitality segment, according to the BLS. As per the visitor statistics maintained by Hawaii’s Department of Business, Economic Development & Tourism, the total passenger count, excluding flights from Canada, has declined on a year-over-year basis in June, July, and the first two weeks of August 2025.

The tourism slowdown in Hawai’i is part of a wider problem in the country. According to a news report citing the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority, tourism in Las Vegas plunged by 11.3% year-over-year in June 2025.

The outlook for business activity, and consequently commercial loan demand, in California and New York is not much better than in Hawai’i. The jobs in these two states have been stagnant this year.

On a positive note, Hope Bancorp has been hiring lately, which is likely to help it capture more market share. As mentioned in the conference call, the management has been hiring commercial and corporate bankers over the past several months. It plans to continue to hire. The management was optimistic that loan production would continue to increase over the rest of the year based on the pipeline at the beginning of the third quarter.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan balance to decline by 0.5% in each of the remaining two quarters of this year.

I’m expecting deposits to grow marginally because of the team expansion and tariff-driven inflation. NOMINAL deposits will rise as inflation picks up pace. Due to the mismatch between loan and deposit growth, I believe Hope Bancorp will have to park the funds raised through deposits in securities. The following table shows my estimates for loans, deposits, and other balance sheet items (Other Earning Assets are mostly securities).

Repositioned Securities Portfolio, Acquisition to Support the Margin

The net interest margin jumped up by 15 basis points in 2Q’25. One of the major reasons for the improvement was a repositioning of the securities portfolio. As I mentioned above, Hope Bancorp sold AFS securities at a loss of around $39 million. The company redeployed the sale proceeds into higher-yielding securities. The management mentioned in the conference call that the impact of this repositioning will likely contribute approximately $12 million per year to interest income.

Another reason for the margin’s improvement was the acquisition of Territorial Bancorp. Hope Bancorp reported accretion income totaling $4 million from the acquisition in the second quarter. The management mentioned in the conference call that the accretion income will be a recurring stable component of the interest income due to the long-dated nature of Territorial loans. For 2025, the management expects to recognize approximately $12 million of loan accretion income at around $4 million per quarter. Previously, the management had anticipated accretion income of around $14 million for 2025.

Contrary to the above two factors, the Fed funds rate cuts can hurt the margin in the remainder of the year. I’m expecting the Fed to cut rates by 25 basis points in either September or October, and then cut rates by another 25 basis points in December of this year. Currently, the balance sheet is asset sensitive, meaning the re-pricing downward of loans will likely outweigh the re-pricing downward of deposits following rate cuts. The management’s rate simulation model shows that a 100-basis-point rate cut could reduce the net interest income by 2.0%.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by 4 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Expecting Normalized EPS of $0.60

Hope Bancorp reported a loss per share of $0.22 in 2Q’25 because of the sale of securities and one-time merger-related expenses. On a normalized basis, the loss per share was $0.06, according to Seeking Alpha. As the second quarter’s notable items are unlikely to recur, earnings will likely turn back to black from the third quarter onwards.

Further, the slight margin expansion will support the bottom line. Additionally, the growth of securities on the back of deposit growth will help the earnings.

After the spike in 2Q’25 because of the acquisition, I’m expecting the provision-expense-to-loan ratio to return to the 1Q’25 level for the remainder of the year. To forecast the non-interest income and non-interest expense, I’m taking the management’s guidance, excluding notable items, given in the presentation.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report an EPS of $0.45 for 2025. Further, I’m expecting normalized EPS of $0.60.

HOPE is Offering an Attractive Dividend Yield of 5.4%

Hope Bancorp has maintained its quarterly dividend at $0.14 per share since the third quarter of 2018. This dividend level suggests quite an attractive dividend yield of 5.4%. Even though the implied payout ratio for 2025 is above 100% at 123%, I think the chances of a dividend cut are very low. Hope Bancorp can easily afford to pay more dividends than its earnings for at least one year. The company reported a total capital ratio of 13.76% for June-end 2025, which was comfortably above the minimum regulatory requirement of 10.50%. Hope Bancorp’s excess capital was $495 million (calculated by me using capital ratios given in the 10-Q filing), while the total annual dividend cost, at a rate of $0.56 per share ($0.14 per share times four), comes out to just $72 million.

Maintaining a Hold Rating

I last wrote a report on Hope Bancorp back in May 2024, wherein I adopted a hold rating on the stock. Since then, the stock price has dipped by around 6.0% and the stock has given a total return of positive 1.3%, so I’m satisfied with my call. To arrive at a new target price, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples given below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my normalized EPS forecast gives a target price of $6.4 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $12.7 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $9.6, which suggests a 7.1% downside from the August 15 closing market price. Adding the dividend yield of 5.4% to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 1.7%. Hence, I’m going to adopt a hold rating on Hope Bancorp once again.

After posting a loss in the latest quarter, Hope Bancorp’s earnings are set to turn back to black. The bottom line will receive support from some margin expansion, plus the growth of securities. Hope Bancorp also offers an attractive dividend yield. Unfortunately, the stock price appears a bit high relative to the fair value, which leads to a slightly negative total expected return.

data.title: Why I Recently Bought Energy Transfer Instead Of Genesis Energy
data.description:
As most of my regular readers know, midstream infrastructure (AMLP) is my favorite place to invest right now because it combines attractive yields with substantial underlying cash flow coverage, defensive business models with long-duration contracts that are largely commodity price resistant, and durable business models that benefit in many respects from the ongoing energy boom in the United States due to the combination of reshoring of industrial activity with the AI boom. On top of that, many midstream contracts have inflation-linked escalators, while energy itself is often viewed as an attractive inflation hedge, positioning the sector well to weather a period of prolonged stagflation.

Midstream companies also have some of the strongest balance sheets among high-yield companies, with well-laddered debt maturities and relatively low leverage. Last but not least, most midstream businesses are growing their payouts at rates that meet or beat inflation, and in some cases, they are even buying back equity.

Given my bullish take on the sector, I was recently looking to add a new position to my portfolio to further diversify my midstream holdings. In my search, I strongly considered both Energy Transfer (ET) and Genesis Energy (GEL), but ultimately settled on adding ET to my portfolio. In this article, I will detail why.

Reason #1

A big reason why I favor ET over GEL is that ET is much more conservatively positioned. For example, its balance sheet leverage ratio is around 4.0 times, which is at the low end of its leverage ratio target. GEL has a similar 4.0 times leverage ratio target, but it is still far away from achieving that, with a leverage ratio of 5.52 times, and management indicated on its latest earnings call that it would be a while before they achieved their leverage target.

As a result, it should be little surprise that ET has a credit rating of BBB from S&P, whereas GEL is not investment-grade rated. On top of that, ET’s business model is much better diversified, with assets spanning across the midstream value chain, including a large and growing exposure to both natural gas and natural gas liquids. In fact, roughly half of its 2025 growth capital is expected to be spent on natural gas-focused projects, while its NGL export business is also competitively positioned and likely to grow as demand from foreign countries for U.S. NGL is booming.

In contrast, GEL has a much smaller business that is focused primarily on its offshore pipeline transportation assets and is supplemented by marine transportation and onshore transportation services. While these are quality businesses, they are simply much smaller in size and have much lower diversification, and thereby less safety and long-term growth opportunities than ET has. In particular, ET, through its natural gas assets, is positioned to benefit from the electrification trend, including data center demand in the United States, which gives it a lengthy growth profile.

Moreover, its geographic and commodity diversification is positioned to grow via strategic bolt-on acquisitions and organic growth projects at attractive risk-adjusted returns.

Reason #2

However, what really convinced me to buy ET instead of GEL is that with ET, you are buying an existing high-yield distribution with a better-known growth profile, whereas with GEL, you are buying with the hope that they will be able to ramp up their distribution. Yet, you do not get any valuation discount for that speculative bet on their ability to deleverage and hike the distribution.

For example, if you look at their forecasted distribution growth, analysts expect GEL to grow at a 10% CAGR through the end of the decade, which is quite substantial.

However, the unit price between ET and GEL is very close right now, with GEL trading at $16.90 and ET trading at $17.24 as of this writing. And yet, today, ET is expected to pay out a $1.63 distribution in 2029, whereas GEL is only expected to pay out a $0.97 distribution in 2029.

On top of that, GEL’s distributable cash flow per unit is expected to come in at $2.92 in 2029, while ET’s is expected to come in at $3.15 at the end of 2029, despite ET also paying out a much more substantial distribution along the way. Beyond 2029, I expect ET to have much greater growth potential, and in fact, analysts even see this playing out before then, with the vast majority of GEL’s growth coming through 2026 as it ramps up its free cash flow generation. Between 2027 and 2029, however, it is only expected to grow its distributable cash flow per unit at a 4.9% CAGR, whereas ET is expected to grow its distributable cash flow per unit at a 5.7% CAGR.

Given its larger scale and geographic diversification, ET should be able to sustain a higher growth rate than GEL over the long term.

When you couple that generally more attractive overall distribution and cash flow growth profile with the fact that ET today yields 7.9% on a next-12-month basis versus 4.1% on a next-12-month basis for GEL, and ET’s EV to EBITDA is 8.1 times compared to GEL’s 10.1 times EV to EBITDA, ET looks like a much better risk-adjusted opportunity. Even if GEL can successfully deliver on its growth of the distribution—which also implies it will be successful in achieving a significant reduction in its leverage moving forward—ET is still likely going to look better relative to current costs by the end of the decade. That is not even accounting for the fact that investors in ET today will be receiving significantly more in distributions over that time, which they can in turn reinvest into buying additional units of ET to further enhance the gap in total distribution income by the end of the decade relative to investors in GEL today.

Investor Takeaway

Therefore, whether you are looking at ET’s much better-diversified business model, lower leverage and higher credit rating, exposure to major growth avenues in natural gas and NGLs (which GEL lacks), or ET’s higher current yield and more favorable distribution and distributable cash flow outlook through the end of the decade and likely beyond relative to GEL, it appears that ET is a slam-dunk choice over GEL.

Perhaps one advantage that GEL does have is that its potential to grow its distribution significantly in the near term could serve as an additional catalyst for unit prices, as the market tends to get excited about substantial distribution growth—as ET itself experienced several years ago after slashing its distribution, only to grow it back quite aggressively, leading to a soaring unit price in the process. However, GEL has already largely seen this uplift in its unit price as the market has anticipated its upcoming inflection point, and it is therefore likely priced in already. In fact, based on analyst consensus estimates as well as current valuation metrics, it appears that Mr. Market may have overpriced this in, at least relative to ET.

As a result, I am steering clear of GEL and have added ET to my personal portfolio as a nice complement to the several other blue-chip, high-yielding, and attractively growing midstream positions in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.
